Definition: A monitoring and observability service — collecting metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources and applications. Provides dashboards, alarms, anomaly detection, and composite alarms for comprehensive operational visibility across your environment.

Numerical data points published to CloudWatch — default metrics (CPU, network, disk) at 5-minute intervals for free, detailed monitoring at 1-minute intervals (paid). Custom metrics via PutMetricData API with standard (60s) or high-resolution (1s) granularity.

Automated actions triggered by metric thresholds — states: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA. Actions: SNS notification, Auto Scaling, EC2 actions (stop/terminate/reboot/recover). Composite alarms combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic to reduce alarm noise.

A centralized log management service — collecting logs from EC2 (via CloudWatch Agent), Lambda, ECS, Route 53, VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and custom sources. Features: log groups, log streams, metric filters, subscription filters, Logs Insights for querying, and cross-account log sharing.

An interactive query language for analyzing log data — running queries across log groups with fields, filter, stats, sort, and parse commands. Automatically discovers fields in JSON logs. Used for troubleshooting, pattern analysis, and operational intelligence.

A unified agent installed on EC2/on-premises servers — collecting system-level metrics (memory, disk, swap, processes) not available by default, plus custom application logs. Configured via JSON config file, supports both metrics and logs collection simultaneously.

Customizable visualization pages — displaying metrics, logs, and alarms from multiple AWS accounts and regions in a single view. Supports automatic dashboards, cross-account dashboards, and sharing via public URLs or within Organizations.

ML-powered feature that creates a model of expected metric behavior — establishing a band of normal values and alerting when metrics fall outside. Useful for metrics with seasonal patterns or trends where static thresholds are impractical.

Configurable scripts that run on a schedule to monitor endpoints and APIs — simulating user behavior, checking availability, latency, and UI screenshots. Detects issues before customers do by proactively testing application health.

An observability feature integrating CloudWatch with X-Ray traces and service maps — providing a unified view of application health, performance bottlenecks, and impacted users by correlating metrics, logs, and traces.

A distributed tracing service — analyzing and debugging microservices architectures by tracing requests as they travel through application components. Provides service maps, trace analysis, latency histograms, and error/fault identification across services.

Segments (data about work done by a service), Subsegments (granular timing for downstream calls), Traces (end-to-end request path), Annotations (indexed key-value pairs for filtering), Metadata (non-indexed additional data), and Sampling rules (controlling trace volume).

A governance and audit service logging all API calls — Management events (control plane, free), Data events (S3 object/Lambda, paid), and Insights events (unusual API activity detection). Trails deliver logs to S3 with optional CloudWatch Logs integration.

An automated anomaly detection feature — identifying unusual API call volumes, error rates, and patterns by establishing baselines and alerting on deviations. Helps detect operational issues like accidental resource deletion or unauthorized access attempts.

Capturing IP traffic information for VPC network interfaces — at VPC, subnet, or ENI level. Records source/destination IPs, ports, protocol, packets, bytes, and action (ACCEPT/REJECT). Published to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose for analysis.

A serverless event bus for event-driven architectures — routing events from AWS services, SaaS apps, and custom sources to targets based on rules. Features: schema registry, archive/replay, cross-account delivery, scheduled rules, and input transformation.

Provides personalized alerts about AWS service events affecting your resources — Personal Health Dashboard (your account-specific events) and Service Health Dashboard (general AWS service status). Integrates with EventBridge for automated remediation.

A fully managed Grafana service for operational dashboards — querying, visualizing, and alerting on metrics from CloudWatch, Prometheus, X-Ray, and other data sources. Supports SAML/SSO authentication and workspace-level access control.

A serverless Prometheus-compatible monitoring service — collecting, storing, and querying container metrics at scale using PromQL. Integrates with EKS, ECS, and self-managed Kubernetes for container workload monitoring.

Design principles for reliable systems — automatically recover from failure, test recovery procedures, scale horizontally, stop guessing capacity, and manage change through automation. Covers foundations, workload architecture, change management, and failure management.

Deploying resources across multiple Availability Zones for high availability — each AZ is one or more data centers with independent power, cooling, and networking. Services like RDS Multi-AZ, ELB, and Auto Scaling natively support multi-AZ deployment.

Deploying across multiple AWS Regions for disaster recovery and global performance — using Route 53 for DNS failover, S3 Cross-Region Replication, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, and CloudFront for content delivery.

Four strategies with increasing cost and decreasing RTO/RPO — Backup & Restore (hours RTO/RPO), Pilot Light (minutes RTO, core running), Warm Standby (minutes RTO, scaled-down copy), Multi-Site Active-Active (near-zero RTO/RPO, full duplicate).

Recovery Point Objective (RPO): maximum acceptable data loss measured in time — how far back you can afford to lose. Recovery Time Objective (RTO): maximum acceptable downtime — how quickly you must recover. These drive DR strategy selection and cost.

A centralized backup service — managing backups for EC2, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, S3, and more. Features: backup plans (schedules, retention, lifecycle), cross-region/cross-account backup, vault lock (WORM compliance), and audit manager.

A service for minimizing downtime and data loss with affordable DR — continuously replicating on-premises or cloud servers to AWS using block-level replication. Enables point-in-time recovery with RPO of seconds and RTO of minutes.

Automatically adjusting capacity to maintain performance and minimize costs — EC2 Auto Scaling (instances), Application Auto Scaling (DynamoDB, ECS, Aurora replicas, Lambda concurrency), and Predictive Scaling (ML-based forecasting for proactive scaling).

Monitoring target health to route traffic only to healthy instances — HTTP/HTTPS/TCP checks with configurable interval, threshold, and timeout. Unhealthy targets are removed from rotation; Auto Scaling can replace failed instances based on ELB health checks.

DNS-based failover between primary and secondary resources — using health checks to detect primary failure and automatically routing traffic to secondary (another region, static S3 website, or CloudFront). Active-passive or active-active configurations.

Automated daily backups with transaction logs (point-in-time recovery up to 5-minute granularity, 0-35 day retention) and manual snapshots (user-initiated, persist until deleted). Cross-region snapshot copy for DR. Aurora backups are continuous to S3.

Versioning protects against accidental deletion/overwrite. Cross-Region Replication (CRR) for DR and compliance. Same-Region Replication (SRR) for log aggregation. S3 Object Lock (WORM) for regulatory compliance with Governance and Compliance modes.

Limiting the impact of failures — using multiple AZs, microservices architecture, bulkhead pattern (isolating components), circuit breaker pattern (preventing cascade failures), and shuffle sharding (assigning resources to overlapping but distinct groups).

Deliberately injecting failures to test system resilience — AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) provides controlled experiments like stopping instances, throttling APIs, and injecting latency to validate recovery procedures and identify weaknesses.

A managed chaos engineering service — running controlled experiments that inject faults (EC2 stop/terminate, network disruption, API throttling, AZ outage) with safety guardrails (stop conditions) to validate system resilience and recovery.

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) service — defining AWS resources in JSON/YAML templates, deploying as stacks with automatic dependency ordering, rollback on failure, drift detection, change sets for preview, nested stacks, and cross-stack references via exports.

Templates (resource definitions), Stacks (deployed template instances), Change Sets (preview changes before applying), Drift Detection (identify manual changes), Stack Sets (deploy across accounts/regions), and StackSets with Organizations integration.

Automatic rollback on stack creation/update failure — preserving the previous working state. Options: disable rollback for debugging, continue rollback for stuck resources, and stack policies to prevent accidental updates to critical resources.

Deploying CloudFormation stacks across multiple accounts and regions in a single operation — using service-managed (Organizations) or self-managed permissions. Supports automatic deployment to new accounts added to an OU.

An IaC framework using familiar programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Java, C#, Go) — defining cloud infrastructure with constructs that synthesize into CloudFormation templates. Higher-level abstractions than raw CloudFormation.

A service for creating and managing approved IT service catalogs — administrators define products (CloudFormation templates) in portfolios, users launch pre-approved resources with governance constraints, TagOptions, and launch constraints.

A unified management hub for AWS and on-premises resources — providing Run Command, Session Manager, Patch Manager, State Manager, Parameter Store, Automation, Inventory, and Maintenance Windows for operational management at scale.

Remotely executing commands on managed instances without SSH/RDP — using documents (predefined or custom scripts) across fleets of instances with rate control, error thresholds, and output to S3/CloudWatch. No bastion hosts or open inbound ports needed.

A browser-based interactive shell and CLI access to EC2 instances — no SSH keys, no bastion hosts, no open inbound ports. Full audit logging to S3/CloudWatch, IAM-based access control, and port forwarding support.

Automated patching for managed instances — using patch baselines (approved/rejected patches), maintenance windows (scheduled time), and patch groups (targeting). Supports Windows, Linux, and macOS with compliance reporting.

Maintaining instances in a defined state — using associations that apply configurations (documents) on a schedule. Ensures instances stay compliant with desired configuration (e.g., antivirus installed, specific services running).

Executing runbooks for common maintenance and deployment tasks — predefined or custom runbooks with approval steps, branching logic, and integration with other AWS services. Used for AMI creation, instance remediation, and multi-step workflows.

Defining recurring time windows for running administrative tasks — patching, Run Command, Automation, Lambda, and Step Functions tasks during scheduled periods to minimize disruption to production workloads.

Collecting metadata about managed instances — installed applications, OS details, network configurations, Windows updates, services, and custom inventory. Aggregated in Resource Data Sync to S3 for analysis with Athena and QuickSight.

A configuration management service using Chef or Puppet — OpsWorks Stacks (layers, instances, apps with Chef recipes) and OpsWorks for Chef Automate / Puppet Enterprise (managed servers). Used for complex application configurations.

An automated pipeline for creating, testing, and distributing AMIs and container images — defining image recipes (base image + components), running build/test pipelines on schedule, and distributing to multiple regions and accounts.

In-Place/Rolling (update existing instances), Blue/Green (parallel environments, instant switchover), Canary (small percentage first), Linear (incremental rollout), All-at-Once (fastest, riskiest), and Immutable (new instances, swap ASG). Choose based on risk tolerance and downtime requirements.

A managed deployment service — deploying to EC2, Lambda, and ECS with in-place, blue/green, canary, and linear strategies. Uses appspec.yml for deployment instructions, lifecycle hooks for custom scripts, and automatic rollback on failure.

A managed CI/CD pipeline service — orchestrating source (CodeCommit/GitHub/S3), build (CodeBuild), test, and deploy (CodeDeploy/CloudFormation/ECS/S3) stages with manual approval gates, parallel actions, and cross-region/cross-account deployments.

A fully managed build service — compiling source code, running tests, and producing deployment artifacts. Uses buildspec.yml for build instructions, supports custom Docker images, caches dependencies, and scales automatically with no build servers to manage.

A PaaS for deploying web applications — handling provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and monitoring. Supports Java, .NET, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Docker. Deployment policies: All at Once, Rolling, Rolling with Additional Batch, Immutable, Traffic Splitting.

Term: EC2 User Data and Launch Templates
Definition: User Data: scripts executed on first boot for instance initialization (install software, configure settings). Launch Templates: versioned configurations specifying AMI, instance type, key pair, security groups, and user data — used by Auto Scaling and EC2 Fleet.

Amazon Machine Images as deployment artifacts — golden AMIs (pre-configured, tested images), AMI lifecycle (build → test → distribute → deprecate), cross-region copy for DR, sharing with other accounts, and EC2 Image Builder for automated pipelines.

Root account: enable MFA, don't use for daily tasks. Use IAM roles over long-term credentials. Enforce least privilege with Access Analyzer. Rotate credentials regularly. Use permission boundaries for delegated administration. Enable CloudTrail for audit.

A service that identifies resources shared with external entities — analyzing S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda functions, and SQS queues for unintended public or cross-account access. Also validates policies against best practices and generates policies from activity.

A service recording and evaluating resource configurations — tracking changes over time, evaluating compliance against rules (managed and custom), triggering remediation actions, and providing a configuration timeline. Aggregators combine data across accounts/regions.

Evaluating resource compliance — Managed rules (pre-built by AWS, e.g., encrypted-volumes, restricted-ssh), Custom rules (Lambda-based), and Conformance Packs (collections of rules as a single entity). Trigger types: configuration change or periodic evaluation.

Automatically fixing non-compliant resources — using SSM Automation documents as remediation actions triggered by Config rule violations. Supports auto-remediation (immediate) and manual remediation (approval required) with retry configuration.

A centralized security posture management service — aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Firewall Manager, and third-party tools. Automated compliance checks against CIS, PCI DSS, and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standards.

An intelligent threat detection service — analyzing CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs, and S3 data events using ML. Detects compromised instances, unauthorized access, cryptocurrency mining, and data exfiltration with severity-rated findings.

An automated vulnerability management service — continuously scanning EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and ECR container images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs) and network exposure. Findings include severity, affected packages, and remediation guidance.

An automated best-practice audit across 5 pillars — Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, and Service Limits. Basic checks free for all; full checks require Business/Enterprise Support. Integrates with EventBridge for automated responses.

Operational considerations — key rotation (automatic annual for AWS-managed, manual for customer-managed), key policies vs IAM policies, grants for temporary access, cross-account key sharing, and monitoring key usage via CloudTrail for security auditing.

Managing SSL/TLS certificates — free public certificates with auto-renewal for ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway. Private CA for internal certificates. Certificate transparency logging, DNS or email validation, and importing third-party certificates.

Block Public Access (account and bucket level), bucket policies for access control, encryption (SSE-S3 default, SSE-KMS for audit), access logging, versioning with MFA Delete, Object Lock for WORM compliance, and VPC endpoints for private access.

A service for setting up and governing multi-account environments — providing landing zones, guardrails (preventive SCPs and detective Config rules), account factory for provisioned accounts, and a dashboard for compliance visibility across the organization.

CloudTrail (API audit), Config (resource compliance), Security Hub (centralized findings), Access Analyzer (external access), Artifact (compliance reports/agreements), and Audit Manager (continuous audit evidence collection mapped to frameworks like SOC 2, PCI DSS).

An isolated virtual network — subnets (public with IGW route, private with NAT), route tables, internet gateway, NAT gateway, VPC peering (non-transitive), Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke), and CIDR planning for non-overlapping address spaces.

Capturing and analyzing network traffic — at VPC, subnet, or ENI level. Key fields: srcaddr, dstaddr, srcport, dstport, protocol, action (ACCEPT/REJECT). Analyze with CloudWatch Logs Insights, Athena (S3), or third-party tools for security and troubleshooting.

A regional network hub connecting VPCs, VPNs, and Direct Connect — supporting transitive routing, route tables for segmentation, multicast, inter-region peering, and centralized network management. Replaces complex VPC peering mesh topologies.

A dedicated private connection from on-premises to AWS — 1/10/100 Gbps dedicated or 50 Mbps-10 Gbps hosted connections. Virtual interfaces: Public (AWS public services), Private (VPC), Transit (Transit Gateway). Use with VPN for encrypted backup.

An encrypted IPsec tunnel over the internet — quick setup (minutes vs weeks for Direct Connect), supports BGP dynamic routing, and can use as primary or backup connection. Accelerated VPN uses Global Accelerator for improved performance.

A global CDN — caching content at 400+ edge locations for low latency. Features: Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3, custom error pages, geo-restriction, signed URLs/cookies for private content, Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions for edge compute.

DNS routing strategies — Simple (single resource), Weighted (traffic splitting by percentage), Latency (nearest region), Failover (active-passive with health checks), Geolocation (by country/continent), Geoproximity (bias-adjustable), Multi-value (multiple healthy records).

Private connectivity to AWS services — Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB, free, route table entry) and Interface endpoints (PrivateLink, ENI-based, security group controlled). Essential for private subnets accessing AWS services without NAT/internet gateway.

ALB (Layer 7, path/host routing), NLB (Layer 4, static IP, ultra-low latency), GLB (Layer 3, appliances). Operational: access logs to S3, connection draining, cross-zone load balancing, slow start mode, sticky sessions, and target group health monitoring.

Common tools and approaches — VPC Flow Logs (traffic analysis), Reachability Analyzer (path analysis without sending traffic), Network Access Analyzer (unintended access), Traffic Mirroring (packet capture), and CloudWatch metrics for network performance.

A managed stateful firewall for VPCs — providing intrusion prevention (IPS), web filtering, protocol detection, and domain-based filtering. Deployed in a firewall subnet with rules managed centrally via Firewall Manager across accounts.

A network diagnostics tool that analyzes path between source and destination — identifying configuration issues (security groups, NACLs, route tables) without sending actual traffic. Provides hop-by-hop analysis showing where connectivity is blocked.

A visualization tool for analyzing spending — viewing cost trends, forecasting future costs, identifying cost drivers by service/account/tag, and receiving RI/Savings Plan purchase recommendations based on historical usage patterns.

An ML-powered service recommending optimal compute resources — analyzing CloudWatch metrics to identify over/under-provisioned EC2 instances, EBS volumes, Lambda functions, and ECS/Fargate tasks with projected cost savings and performance impact.

Identifying cost-saving opportunities — idle load balancers, underutilized EC2 instances, unassociated Elastic IPs, idle RDS instances, low-utilization EBS volumes, and Reserved Instance optimization recommendations.

Matching instance types to workload requirements — using CloudWatch CPU/memory metrics, Compute Optimizer recommendations, and Cost Explorer right-sizing recommendations. Downsize over-provisioned instances and upgrade under-provisioned ones.

Combining pricing models — Reserved Instances/Savings Plans for baseline (up to 72% savings), Spot Instances for fault-tolerant workloads (up to 90% savings), On-Demand for spikes, and Dedicated Hosts for licensing. Auto Scaling mixed instances for cost-optimized fleets.

Lifecycle policies for automatic tiering (Standard → IA → Glacier), Intelligent-Tiering for unknown patterns, S3 Analytics for access pattern insights, S3 Inventory for auditing, and compression/columnar formats to reduce storage volume.

Using CloudWatch metrics, X-Ray traces, and application-level monitoring to identify bottlenecks — CPU, memory, disk I/O, network throughput, and latency. Tune instance types, EBS volume types, caching strategies, and database configurations.

Setting custom cost and usage budgets with alerts — cost, usage, RI utilization, and Savings Plans budgets. Alerts via email/SNS when actual or forecasted spending exceeds thresholds. Automated actions: stop instances, apply SCPs, or trigger Lambda.

Implementing consistent resource tags — cost allocation tags (AWS-generated and user-defined) for billing reports, tag policies in Organizations for enforcement, and Tag Editor for bulk tagging. Essential for chargeback, showback, and cost accountability.

Minimizing transfer charges — use VPC endpoints (free for S3 gateway), CloudFront for outbound, same-AZ placement where possible, S3 Transfer Acceleration for uploads, and Direct Connect for high-volume hybrid transfers. Inbound is always free.
